title: The cleaners who know the office better than the people who work there
summary: Argue what is owed to the night-shift workers who hold the most complete picture of a building yet are the least recognised inside it.
topic: Why the people who clean a workplace at night are simultaneously its most knowledgeable and its most invisible inhabitants
prompt: Write a 450–600-word argumentative essay on a paradox: the night cleaners in an office tower often understand the building — its habits, its waste, its private failures — more completely than the staff who occupy it by day, yet they are the people that staff least recognise. Argue what recognition such workers are owed, and why familiarity has not bought them standing. Give two concrete examples and concede one objection. Use cleft sentences, the passive voice, and concession markers such as 'granted' and 'even so'.
hintStarters: What the daytime staff never see is the version of the building that emerges after…
Granted, knowing a place intimately is not the same as being entitled to…
It is the cleaner, not the manager, who can tell you which desk hides…
We tend to grant recognition to people who are visible, when the deeper knowledge belongs to…

hintVocab.meaningVi: coi ai đó là điều hiển nhiên
công việc bạc bẽo / không được cảm ơn
được thuê khoán qua bên thứ ba
lặng lẽ làm việc, không gây chú ý
thân thiết đến mức gọi tên nhau
thứ bậc / hệ thống cấp bậc
xứng đáng được nhận điều gì

sampleText:
Ask the people who occupy a glass office tower by day what they know about their building, and they will describe meetings, lifts, and a coffee machine. Ask the woman who cleans the fourteenth floor at two in the morning, and she will describe a different place entirely — one that exists only after the badges have been swiped on the way out. What the daytime staff never see is the version of the building that emerges after they leave: the desk where a senior manager keeps a half-finished bottle behind the files, the meeting room whose carpet betrays a leak the facilities team keeps denying, the corner where one team eats lunch alone because no one invites them. This knowledge is real, detailed, and accumulated over years. It is also almost entirely unrecognised.

The paradox is worth stating plainly. It is the cleaner, not the manager, who can tell you which desk hides a private grief and which floor never empties its own bins. She knows the building as a body knows itself, from the inside and after dark. Yet she is the person the daytime staff are least likely to name, greet, or even register. She is subcontracted, she clocks in when they clock out, and she is trained, in effect, to leave no trace — to make the evidence of her labour disappear by morning. The better she does her job, the more invisible it becomes.

Consider a hospital ward in Manila where the same cleaner has worked the night shift for nineteen years. She can read a patient's recovery from the contents of a bin before any chart records it, and the nurses, when pressed, admit they rely on her sense of the ward. None of this appears in her job description or her pay. Or consider an investment bank in London where the daytime traders, asked the name of the man who empties their bins, could not produce it, though he had worked beside them for a decade. He knew all of theirs.

Granted, knowing a place intimately is not the same as being entitled to run it, and no one is arguing that the cleaner should set the firm's strategy. Familiarity is not authority, and to pretend otherwise would be sentimental. The objection is fair: a building has many kinds of expertise, and the one that keeps the lights paying is not nothing.

Even so, the question was never about authority. It was about recognition — the simple acknowledgement that a person who knows your workplace better than you do is a person, with a name, whose labour holds the place together. We tend to grant recognition to people who are visible, when the deeper knowledge belongs to those we are trained not to see. That is the failure worth correcting, and it costs almost nothing to begin: a name learned, a greeting returned, a contract that does not treat the most knowledgeable worker in the building as the most disposable.

What the night cleaner is owed is not promotion or pity. It is standing — the plain civic fact of being counted among the people who make a place work. A building that cannot name the person who knows it best has mistaken being seen for mattering, and quietly decided that the two are the same thing.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: It-clefts to overturn an assumed hierarchy
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'It is the cleaner, not the manager, who can tell you which desk hides a private grief' dùng câu chẻ it-cleft để đẩy người lao công lên vị trí chủ thể của tri thức, đảo ngược thứ bậc mà người đọc mặc định.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'It is the cleaner, not the manager, who can tell you which desk hides a private grief' uses an it-cleft to foreground the cleaner as the one who truly knows, overturning the hierarchy the reader assumes.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive to dramatise enforced invisibility
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'she is trained, in effect, to leave no trace' và 'after the badges have been swiped' dùng bị động để cho thấy sự vô hình của người lao công là điều bị áp đặt lên họ, chứ không phải lựa chọn của họ.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'she is trained, in effect, to leave no trace' and 'after the badges have been swiped' use the passive to show the cleaner's invisibility as something done to her rather than chosen by her.

sampleTranslationVi:
Hãy hỏi những người chiếm giữ một tòa tháp văn phòng kính vào ban ngày xem họ biết gì về tòa nhà của mình, và họ sẽ kể về các cuộc họp, thang máy, và một cái máy pha cà phê. Hãy hỏi người phụ nữ dọn dẹp tầng mười bốn vào lúc hai giờ sáng, và bà sẽ mô tả một nơi hoàn toàn khác — một nơi chỉ tồn tại sau khi thẻ ra vào đã được quẹt lúc đi về. Cái mà nhân viên ban ngày không bao giờ thấy là phiên bản tòa nhà hiện ra sau khi họ rời đi: cái bàn nơi một quản lý cấp cao giấu một chai rượu uống dở sau đống hồ sơ, phòng họp có tấm thảm để lộ một chỗ rò mà đội cơ sở vật chất cứ chối, góc phòng nơi một nhóm ăn trưa một mình vì chẳng ai mời họ. Sự hiểu biết này là thật, chi tiết, và được tích lũy qua nhiều năm. Nó cũng gần như hoàn toàn không được công nhận.

Nghịch lý này đáng được nói thẳng ra. Chính người lao công, không phải người quản lý, mới có thể cho bạn biết bàn làm việc nào giấu một nỗi đau riêng và tầng nào không bao giờ tự đổ thùng rác của mình. Bà hiểu tòa nhà như một cơ thể hiểu chính mình, từ bên trong và sau khi trời tối. Vậy mà bà là người mà nhân viên ban ngày ít có khả năng gọi tên, chào hỏi, hay thậm chí nhận ra nhất. Bà làm việc theo hợp đồng thuê khoán, bà chấm công vào ca khi họ chấm công ra về, và trên thực tế, bà được rèn để không để lại dấu vết nào — để làm cho bằng chứng về lao động của mình biến mất trước khi trời sáng. Bà làm việc càng tốt thì công việc ấy càng trở nên vô hình.

Hãy xét đến một khu bệnh ở Manila nơi cùng một người lao công đã làm ca đêm suốt mười chín năm. Bà có thể đọc được sự hồi phục của bệnh nhân từ những thứ trong thùng rác trước khi bất kỳ bệnh án nào ghi lại, và các y tá, khi bị gặng hỏi, thừa nhận rằng họ dựa vào cảm nhận của bà về khu bệnh. Không điều nào trong số này xuất hiện trong bản mô tả công việc hay tiền lương của bà. Hoặc hãy xét đến một ngân hàng đầu tư ở London nơi các nhà giao dịch ban ngày, khi được hỏi tên người đàn ông đổ thùng rác cho họ, không thể nói ra được, dù ông đã làm việc bên cạnh họ suốt một thập kỷ. Ông biết tên tất cả bọn họ.

Công nhận, hiểu tường tận một nơi không giống với việc có quyền điều hành nó, và chẳng ai cho rằng người lao công nên đặt ra chiến lược của công ty. Sự quen thuộc không phải là quyền lực, và giả vờ ngược lại thì sẽ là ủy mị. Phản đối này là công bằng: một tòa nhà có nhiều loại chuyên môn, và loại giữ cho đèn được trả tiền thì không phải là vô nghĩa.

Dẫu vậy, vấn đề chưa bao giờ là về quyền lực. Nó là về sự công nhận — sự thừa nhận đơn giản rằng một người hiểu nơi làm việc của bạn rõ hơn cả bạn là một con người, có tên gọi, mà lao động của họ giữ cho nơi ấy gắn kết. Chúng ta có xu hướng dành sự công nhận cho những người dễ thấy, trong khi sự hiểu biết sâu hơn lại thuộc về những người mà ta được rèn để không nhìn thấy. Đó là cái sai đáng được sửa, và nó tốn gần như không gì để bắt đầu: một cái tên được học, một lời chào được đáp lại, một hợp đồng không coi người lao động hiểu biết nhất trong tòa nhà là người dễ bị thải loại nhất.

Cái mà người lao công đêm xứng đáng được nhận không phải là sự thăng tiến hay lòng thương hại. Đó là vị thế — sự thật công dân giản dị của việc được tính vào số những người làm cho một nơi vận hành. Một tòa nhà không thể gọi tên người hiểu nó rõ nhất đã nhầm lẫn việc được nhìn thấy với việc có ý nghĩa, và lặng lẽ quyết định rằng hai điều ấy là một.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses an it-cleft to overturn the assumed hierarchy of who knows the building?
options: It is the cleaner, not the manager, who can tell you which desk hides a private grief.
The cleaner can tell you which desk it is the manager hides a private grief.
Which desk hides a private grief is it the cleaner not the manager telling you.
Tell you the cleaner it is not the manager which desk a private grief hides.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'It is X, not Y, who …' is an it-cleft that foregrounds the cleaner as the true holder of knowledge.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses the passive to show the cleaner's invisibility as imposed, not chosen?
options: She chooses to leave no trace because she likes hiding her work.
She is trained, in effect, to leave no trace.
Leaving no trace is the thing she trains herself for the liking of it.
No trace is what she leaves trained herself in effect to.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'She is trained … to leave no trace' is passive and frames the invisibility as done to her.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option uses 'is + past participle' and removes her own agency?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence introduces the counter-argument most fairly and naturally?
options: Obviously cleaners should just be grateful they have any job at all.
Granted, knowing a place intimately is not the same as being entitled to run it.
Of course no cleaner could ever understand a real business.
Anyone who disagrees with me clearly has never cleaned anything.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted, …' concedes a real limit (knowledge is not authority) without insulting anyone.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option admits a fair point against the writer's own thesis?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'invisible labour' mean as used here?
options: Work done by machines instead of people
Real, valuable work that goes unseen and unacknowledged
Work that is illegal or hidden from inspectors
Imaginary work that nobody actually does
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Invisible labour' is genuine effort that holds a place together yet is not noticed.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of work that is real but never seen.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best pivots from the concession back to the main argument?
options: In conclusion, cleaners should run every company.
Even so, the question was never about authority. It was about recognition.
On the contrary, knowledge means nothing at all.
Anyway, none of this really matters in the end.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so, …' accepts the concession about authority and redirects to the real claim about recognition.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase accepts the prior point and re-centres the thesis?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence keeps a consistent argumentative register?
options: Honestly it's just so sad how nobody cares about cleaners, right?
What the night cleaner is owed is not promotion or pity. It is standing.
Basically the cleaner knows everything and the bosses are all clueless idiots.
It kind of sucks that people don't say hi to the cleaning lady, you know?
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B states the claim precisely and formally; the others slip into casual or insulting speech.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the central paradox the essay argues?
options: Cleaners earn more than managers but feel poorer
The worker who knows the building most completely is the one least recognised within it
Offices are cleaner at night than during the day
Managers secretly do all the cleaning themselves
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay turns on the gap between deep knowledge and near-total invisibility.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses parallel structure most effectively?
options: the person the daytime staff are least likely to name, greet, or even register
the person to name, greeting, and that they even register least likely
the person who naming, to greet, or registered the daytime staff least
the person least the daytime staff name, greet, registering or even likely
mcQuestions.explanation: Three matched bare infinitives ('name, greet, register') form a clean parallel list.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option keeps all three verbs in the same form?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best expresses the essay's claim about why familiarity has not earned recognition?
options: We tend to grant recognition to people who are visible, when the deeper knowledge belongs to those we are trained not to see.
People get recognised because they know more stuff than other people do.
Recognition is given out randomly with no pattern to it whatsoever.
Cleaners are not recognised because they genuinely know nothing useful.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option A names the real mechanism: recognition tracks visibility, not depth of knowledge.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option contrasts 'visible' with 'trained not to see'?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to take someone for granted' mean here?
options: To pay someone generously for their work
To assume someone's labour will always be there without valuing it
To promote someone unexpectedly
To greet someone by their first name
mcQuestions.explanation: It means failing to appreciate effort that is assumed to be constant.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of relying on someone while never noticing them.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option uses an em-dash insertion correctly to gloss a noun?
options: It is about recognition — the simple acknowledgement that a person holds the place together.
It is about — recognition the simple — acknowledgement holds together a person.
Recognition it is — about the simple — that a person acknowledgement holds.
Holds the place — it is about recognition — a person the simple together.
mcQuestions.explanation: The dashes enclose an appositive gloss; remove it and a full sentence remains.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option lets you delete the dashed phrase and keep a complete sentence?

mcQuestions.prompt: What, according to the essay, is the cleaner actually owed?
options: A promotion to management
Standing — being counted among the people who make the place work
Pity from the daytime staff
The authority to set company strategy
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay explicitly says not promotion or pity, but standing.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'subcontracted' mean as used here?
options: Employed directly by the company on its own payroll
Hired through a separate outside firm rather than by the company itself
Working without any contract at all
Promoted to a supervisory role
mcQuestions.explanation: A subcontracted worker is employed via a third-party firm, which deepens their distance from the staff.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of work outsourced to an outside company.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best captures the essay's closing reframe?
options: A company that ignores its cleaners will always lose money eventually.
A building that cannot name the person who knows it best has mistaken being seen for mattering.
Cleaners are the only people who matter in any building.
Being seen and mattering are completely unrelated to each other.
mcQuestions.explanation: The closing line names the confusion of visibility with worth — the essay's core idea.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the line that equates 'being seen' with 'mattering' as a mistake.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option avoids overstating the argument while still defending the cleaner?
options: Cleaners are smarter than everyone else and should run the world.
Familiarity is not authority, and to pretend otherwise would be sentimental.
Nobody who works in an office has ever known anything at all.
Knowing a building means you automatically deserve to control it.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B concedes a limit honestly, which strengthens rather than weakens the case for recognition.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option admits knowledge alone does not equal power?

options: A job that pays extremely well
Necessary work for which one receives little or no gratitude
A job that requires no skill
A job that one is grateful to have
mcQuestions.explanation: A thankless job is valuable work that goes unappreciated.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of effort that earns no thanks.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a place you spend your days in — an office, a campus, a building. Could you name the person who cleans it after you leave? If not, what would have to change about how that place is organised for their knowledge of it to count as much as yours?
